title: Test approach for auto-fill missing horn

content.text:
Discuss with me to write the test "expect State.add auto-fill missing horn in valid cases" after the test for verify literal switch in @src/liblex.zig 

- Prove that the implementation will auto-fill missing horn in all possible cases.
- Use @specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md as source of truth.
- Use existing tests as reference for code pattern, convention and technique.
- The feature is not yet implemented. We do test first.

Come up with the approach, then consult the Oracle for another perspective, but prevent the Oracle from producing any example code. You should not write any code.

content.thinking: I need to look at the relevant files to understand what I'm working with.

result.content:
1: # Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method
2: 
3: The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.
4: 
5: ## Vietnamese Telex Rules
6: 
7: ### Diacritics
8: 
9: Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
10: Tones have a separate section.
11: 
12: #### Empty
13: 
14: - Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
15: - Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
16:   characters.
17: 
18: #### Circumflex
19: 
20: - Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.
21: 
22: #### Breve
23: 
24: - Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.
25: 
26: #### Horn
27: 
28: - Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
29: 
30: #### Stroke
31: 
32: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
33: 
34: #### Apply rules
35: 
36: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
37: - _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
38:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
39:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
40:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
41: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
42:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
43:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
44:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
45: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
46: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
47: 
48: #### Override rules
49: 
50: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
51:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
52:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
53: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
54: - Keep tone untouched.
55: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
56: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
57: 
58: #### Cancel rules
59: 
60: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
61:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
62: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
63:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
64:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
65:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
66:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
67: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
68: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
69:   remains until the new word.
70:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
71: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
72: 
73: #### Auto-fill rules
74: 
75: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
76:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
77: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
78:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
79:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
80:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
81: - The new input character is appended literally.
82: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
83: 
84: ### Tones
85: 
86: Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
87: Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
88: These vowel could take any tone.
89: 
90: #### Level (ngang)
91: 
92: - The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
93:   corresponding variants.
94: - If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
95: 
96: #### Rising (sắc)
97: 
98: - Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
99: - Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
100:   -> `Ớ`.
101: 
102: #### Falling (huyền)
103: 
104: - Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
105: - Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
106:   -> `Ờ`.
107: 
108: #### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
109: 
110: - Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
111: - Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
112:   `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
113: 
114: #### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
115: 
116: - Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
117: - Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
118:   -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
119: 
120: #### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
121: 
122: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
123: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
124:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
125: 
126: #### Apply rules
127: 
128: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
129:   character literally.
130: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
131:   their lower cases.
132: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
133: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
134: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
135:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
136: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
137: 
138: #### Cancel rules
139: 
140: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
141:   character literally.
142: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
143:   their lower cases.
144: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
145: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
146: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
147:   the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
148: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
149:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
150: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
151: 
152: #### Reset rules
153: 
154: - Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
155:   character literally.
156: - Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
157: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
158: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
159:   change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
160: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowel and all of them don't have
161:   any tone (level), append the new character literally.
162: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
163:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
164: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
165: 
166: #### Override rules
167: 
168: - Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
169:   character literally.
170: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
171:   their lower cases.
172: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
173: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
174: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
175:   from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
176:   the word. The override tone is always successful.
177: 
178: #### Scan pseudo-word
179: 
180: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
181:   the tone in the correct position.
182: - The existing characters must not be empty.
183: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
184: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
185:   character to scan, then stop.
186: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
187:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
188: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
189:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
190:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
191: 
192: #### Tone position rules
193: 
194: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
195: 
196: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
197:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
198: - Exceptions:
199:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
200: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
201: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
202:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on `Ơ`.
203:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the listed vowels,
204:     prioritize the first to the last in the list.
205:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
206:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
207:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
208:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
209:     consonants, put on second vowel.
210:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
211: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
212: - Could not put tone for other cases.

content.thinking: I need to look at the existing test patterns in the file, especially the verify literal switch test that comes before the one I'm writing.

I'm working through the auto-fill horn feature logic, which applies when a syllable with a missing horn mark precedes new user input—specifically when the input is one of the consonants C, I, M, N, P, T, U. The feature needs to determine which character in the syllable (either the U or O) should receive the horn diacritic based on the new input being typed.

I need to account for various case combinations, tone marks that could appear on either character, and ensure the existing tone and case are preserved while the new input is appended literally. Let me map out all the valid existing pair patterns to handle this comprehensively. Now I'm thinking through test cases to validate the auto-fill behavior across different pair shapes, case combinations, and tone variations. I need to cover both `uơ` and `ưo` patterns with their uppercase variants, test all six tones on the character receiving the horn diacritic, and verify that all seven trigger characters (C, I, M, N, P, T, U) work correctly in both cases, potentially with consonants preceding the pair. Both vowels end up with horns after auto-fill, so I need to decide how to handle tones in the test matrix. Since Vietnamese typically places a tone on only one vowel per syllable, I should either test with tone on the vowel that already has the horn, or on the one receiving it, or both scenarios. Testing both would create a large matrix (6 tones × 6 tones × 8 case combos × 14 triggers), so I'll focus on placing the tone on whichever vowel is getting the horn added, to verify the tone is preserved through the auto-fill operation.

Setting up a test structure with inline comptime loops to iterate through tone variants, case combinations, and trigger characters — similar to the existing `apply_circumflex` test pattern of 72 cases (12 × 6). I should also test with a leading consonant to verify the implementation checks the last two spans rather than just assuming a fixed buffer length. For the test coverage, I'm planning to iterate through the pair patterns where either character is missing the horn diacritic, apply different case combinations, and then systematically vary which character receives the tone mark while keeping the other at the base level — that gives me 12 tone scenarios per pair pattern, multiplied by the trigger characters and case variants to build out comprehensive coverage.

content.text:
I now have the full picture. Let me lay out my approach, then ask the Oracle for a critique.

## My proposed approach

### Goal of the test
Prove that `State.add`, when given a trigger character (`C/I/M/N/P/T/U` + lowercase), inspects the immediately-preceding two spans, and if they form an `uơ` (`u` + `ơ`) or `ưo` (`ư` + `o`) pair, fills the missing horn so the pair becomes `ươ`, while preserving case and tone of both originals and appending the trigger literally.

### Coverage matrix (one inline-for sweep)

1. Pair shape (which character is missing horn):
   - `uơ`: first span is `U/u` with `.empty`, second span is `O/o` with `.horn`. Auto-fill on the first span.
   - `ưo`: first span is `U/u` with `.horn`, second span is `O/o` with `.empty`. Auto-fill on the second span.

2. Case combinations per pair: `uo`, `uO`, `Uo`, `UO` → 4 × 2 pair shapes = 8 starting pairs.

3. Tone preservation. To keep this realistic (Vietnamese: a syllable carries one tone) and the comptime quota manageable, sweep 6 tones placed on each of the two vowels in turn, leaving the other at `.level`. That is 12 tone scenarios per pair.

4. Trigger characters: `C/c, I/i, M/m, N/n, P/p, T/t, U/u` → 14.

5. Position: pair at the very start of the buffer (length 2 before trigger). One additional small variant where the pair sits after a single consonant span (e.g. seed `c` + pair) to confirm the rule looks at the last two spans, not at `buffer_length == 2`.

### Per-case assertions
- `buffer_length == seed_length + 1`.
- `buffer_modification_index` equals the index of the span that received the horn (`seed_length - 2` for `uơ`, `seed_length - 1` for `ưo`).
- `literal_index == null`.
- `mode == .telex`.
- Receiving-character span: same base (case preserved), `diacritic == .horn`, tone preserved.
- Other-character span: same base (case preserved), `diacritic == .horn` (unchanged), tone preserved.
- New trailing span: base equals trigger (case preserved), `.empty`, `.level`.

### Conventions to follow (matching existing tests)
- Single test, Arrange/Act/Assert comments.
- Build the case matrix as comptime tuples, drive with nested `inline for`.
- Bump `@setEvalBranchQuota` (existing largest case uses 2000; this matrix is bigger so likely larger).
- Seed each span only via `Span.init_diacritic_tone` (never mutate `Span`).
- Fresh `var state: State = undefined; state.init(); state.mode = .telex;` per case.

Now consulting the Oracle.

input.task:
Critique my plan for a new Zig test in src/liblex.zig named: "expect State.add auto-fill missing horn in valid cases". The feature is not yet implemented — this is test-first. Do NOT produce any code, pseudocode, or code snippets in your reply. I want only conceptual review: gaps in coverage, redundancies, mis-assertions, edge cases I might be missing, and whether the assertions truly prove the feature in all valid cases per the spec. If you must point at code, refer to symbols and line numbers only.

Spec excerpt (auto-fill rules from specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md):
- Auto-fill applies only to a `ươ` syllable that sits immediately before the user input, when one of the two characters is missing horn: `uơ` (U missing) or `ưo` (O missing).
- Trigger characters: C, I, M, N, P, T, U (and lowercase).
- Fill examples: `uơn` -> `ươn`, `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
- The new input character is appended literally.
- Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.

My approach:

1. Pair shape variants: `uơ` (first span empty, second has horn) and `ưo` (first has horn, second empty). Auto-fill restores horn on whichever character is missing it; result pair is always `ươ` (with original cases).
2. Case combinations per pair: uo, uO, Uo, UO — 4 case combos per shape, 8 total.
3. Tone preservation: 6 tones swept on each vowel position (the other at .level), so 12 tone scenarios per pair (avoiding implausible double-toned syllables).
4. Trigger characters: 7 bases × 2 cases = 14.
5. Position scenarios: (a) pair at start of buffer (buffer_length == 2 before trigger), (b) pair preceded by a single consonant seed (buffer_length == 3 before trigger), to prove the rule looks at the last two spans and not at buffer_length.

Per-case assertions:
- buffer_length == seed_length + 1.
- buffer_modification_index == index of the span that received the horn.
- literal_index == null.
- mode == .telex.
- Receiving span: base preserved, diacritic == .horn, tone preserved.
- Other vowel span: base preserved, diacritic == .horn (unchanged), tone preserved.
- Trailing new span: base equals trigger, .empty, .level.

Conventions: single test, inline-for over comptime tuples, bump @setEvalBranchQuota, seed via Span.init_diacritic_tone, fresh state per case (.telex mode).

Questions for you:
- Does this cover "all possible valid cases" sufficiently to prove the feature?
- Are there boundary scenarios I should add (e.g. pair sitting at indices 14–15 so buffer_length reaches 16 after the trigger; literal_index already set; existing diacritic already on the trigger position; mode == .literal as a non-trigger case maybe out of scope; what if the buffer is at the limit so the trailing append exceeds it; should we cover trigger U specifically since 'U'/'u' is also a vowel that may interfere with the W-trigger horn rules)?
- Are my assertions sufficient to detect a faulty implementation that, e.g., changes the wrong span, or also strips the existing horn?
- Anything I should test that the spec strictly says is NOT auto-fill (negative cases) — for example pair shape `úo` with rising on `u` and empty on `o` (no horn anywhere), or pair `uo` with both empty, or trigger character not in the list?
- Is splitting the test into multiple smaller tests preferable to one big sweep, considering the file's existing convention?

run.result:
1) TL;DR

Your positive matrix is strong, but it does not yet prove the rule is correctly bounded. Keep the exhaustive “valid cases” sweep, but add a small set of negative and boundary tests, and tighten assertions so you verify untouched spans too. Also keep trigger `U`/`u`; that is a high-value case, not redundant.

2) Recommended approach (simple path)

1. Keep the core positive sweep, but define its scope precisely.
   - Your shape coverage is right: both incomplete `ươ` forms are required.
   - Your case coverage is right: preserving original case on each vowel is part of the spec.
   - Your trigger coverage is right: all 14 trigger forms matter because the implementation will likely land in separate switch arms in `src/liblex.zig` around lines 304, 382, 384, 385, 430, 433, and 434.
   - Your tone idea is mostly right, but be explicit that you are covering linguistically valid one-tone syllables, not every representable engine state.

2. Tighten the per-case assertions.
   - Assert post-length as “pre-length plus 1,” not a seed-derived shorthand that could hide an off-by-one.
   - Assert every pre-existing span in the tested window that should remain unchanged, including any prefix consonant seed.
   - In the prefixed case, assert the modification index is offset by the prefix length, not just “the receiving span.”
   - Conceptually, you want to prove exactly one existing span gains horn, the other vowel span is unchanged, the prefix is unchanged, and the new trigger span is appended literally.

3. Add a small negative suite.
   The main gap in your plan is not positive coverage; it is applicability boundaries. Add at least these:
   - valid incomplete pair exists, but is not immediately before the trigger
   - both vowels have no horn
   - both vowels already have horn
   - wrong diacritic shape involved, such as circumflex instead of horn
   - input character not in the trigger list
   - optional but useful: processing is bypassed when `literal_index` is already set or `mode == .literal`

4. Add one overflow boundary test.
   - A very valuable case is when the incomplete pair sits at effective-buffer indices 14–15 and the trigger append pushes total length to 17.
   - That proves the rule still modifies the existing vowel correctly at the buffer edge and handles the literal append / `literal_index` transition correctly.
   - Without this, a broken implementation can pass your main matrix and still mishandle the only branch that both mutates an old span and appends beyond `buffer_effective`.

5. Split tests by purpose.
   - One exhaustive positive matrix test for valid cases
   - One focused negative-applicability test
   - One focused overflow / state-gating test

3) Rationale and trade-offs

- Your current plan is stronger than necessary on the positive path, but still incomplete overall because it doesn’t prove “only these cases trigger.”
- The highest-leverage additions are negative guards, not more positive permutations.
- The existing file already leans this way:
  - matrix-style positive tests at lines 691–809
  - then a focused interaction test at lines 1000–1137
- So a split suite fits existing convention better than one giant everything-test.

A specific note on tones:
- Your “6 tones on each vowel position with the other at level” is a good practical interpretation of valid syllables.
- But it does duplicate the all-level case.
- It also excludes malformed double-toned states, which the engine data model can represent even if the spec wouldn’t call them valid.
- That is fine if you say so explicitly. If you claim “all possible valid cases,” you are okay. If you claim “all possible representable states,” you are not.

4) Risks and guardrails

- Biggest proof gap: over-eager auto-fill.
  - A buggy implementation that fills on non-triggers, non-immediate pairs, or wrong diacritics would still pass your current positive-only plan.
- Biggest assertion gap: untouched-prefix corruption.
  - In your seeded scenario, if the implementation rewrites an earlier span, your listed assertions may miss it unless you explicitly inspect that prefix span.
- Biggest boundary gap: buffer overflow interaction.
  - Auto-fill is unusual because it both mutates an existing span and appends a literal trigger. That makes the index-16 boundary worth testing directly.
- `literal_index == null` is correct for ordinary successful auto-fill cases, but not for the overflow boundary case. So don’t hard-code that expectation into every scenario.

5) When to consider the advanced path

Use a more orthogonal test design only if the big sweep becomes painful to compile or hard to debug:
- very high branch quota
- slow test compile times
- failure output too noisy to localize quickly

6) Optional advanced path

If compile-time cost gets annoying, reduce positive redundancy before dropping negative coverage:
- keep one exhaustive tone/case/shape sweep with a representative trigger
- add a smaller trigger-membership sweep with representative tone/case
- keep the negative and overflow tests intact

Direct answers to your questions:

- Does this prove all valid cases?
  - It proves the transformation well for valid inputs, but not the applicability boundaries. So: not yet.

- Boundary scenarios to add?
  - Yes: especially the index 14–15 / length 16 boundary.
  - `literal_index` already set and `mode == .literal` are secondary but worthwhile as one representative guard test.
  - Cover trigger `U` specifically: yes, absolutely keep it.

- Are your assertions sufficient?
  - Almost, but not quite. Add assertions for every untouched existing span in the setup window, especially the prefix seed. That closes the main hole.

- Negative cases the spec says should not auto-fill?
  - Yes: no horn anywhere, both horns already present, wrong diacritic shape, non-trigger input, and pair not immediately before the input.

- One big sweep or multiple tests?
  - Multiple tests. Keep one big positive sweep, but split negatives and boundary behavior into smaller focused tests. That is the better fit for this file’s existing style around lines 691–809 and 1000–1137.

result.content:
1: # Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method
2: 
3: The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.
4: 
5: ## Vietnamese Telex Rules
6: 
7: ### Diacritics
8: 
9: Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
10: Tones have a separate section.
11: 
12: #### Empty
13: 
14: - Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
15: - Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
16:   characters.
17: 
18: #### Circumflex
19: 
20: - Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.
21: 
22: #### Breve
23: 
24: - Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.
25: 
26: #### Horn
27: 
28: - Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
29: 
30: #### Stroke
31: 
32: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
33: 
34: #### Apply rules
35: 
36: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
37: - _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
38:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
39:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
40:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
41: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
42:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
43:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
44:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
45: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
46: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
47: 
48: #### Override rules
49: 
50: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
51:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
52:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
53: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
54: - Keep tone untouched.
55: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
56: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
57: 
58: #### Cancel rules
59: 
60: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
61:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
62: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
63:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
64:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
65:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
66:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
67: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
68: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
69:   remains until the new word.
70:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
71: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
72: 
73: #### Auto-fill rules
74: 
75: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
76:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
77: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
78:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
79:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
80:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
81: - The new input character is appended literally.
82: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
83: 
84: ### Tones
85: 
86: Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
87: Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
88: These vowel could take any tone.
89: 
90: #### Level (ngang)
91: 
92: - The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
93:   corresponding variants.
94: - If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
95: 
96: #### Rising (sắc)
97: 
98: - Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
99: - Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
100:   -> `Ớ`.
101: 
102: #### Falling (huyền)
103: 
104: - Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
105: - Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
106:   -> `Ờ`.
107: 
108: #### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
109: 
110: - Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
111: - Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
112:   `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
113: 
114: #### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
115: 
116: - Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
117: - Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
118:   -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
119: 
120: #### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
121: 
122: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
123: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
124:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
125: 
126: #### Apply rules
127: 
128: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
129:   character literally.
130: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
131:   their lower cases.
132: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
133: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
134: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
135:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
136: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
137: 
138: #### Cancel rules
139: 
140: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
141:   character literally.
142: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
143:   their lower cases.
144: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
145: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
146: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
147:   the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
148: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
149:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
150: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
151: 
152: #### Reset rules
153: 
154: - Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
155:   character literally.
156: - Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
157: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
158: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
159:   change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
160: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowel and all of them don't have
161:   any tone (level), append the new character literally.
162: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
163:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
164: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
165: 
166: #### Override rules
167: 
168: - Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
169:   character literally.
170: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
171:   their lower cases.
172: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
173: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
174: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
175:   from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
176:   the word. The override tone is always successful.
177: 
178: #### Scan pseudo-word
179: 
180: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
181:   the tone in the correct position.
182: - The existing characters must not be empty.
183: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
184: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
185:   character to scan, then stop.
186: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
187:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
188: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
189:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
190:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
191: 
192: #### Tone position rules
193: 
194: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
195: 
196: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
197:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
198: - Exceptions:
199:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
200: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
201: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
202:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on `Ơ`.
203:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the listed vowels,
204:     prioritize the first to the last in the list.
205:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
206:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
207:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
208:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
209:     consonants, put on second vowel.
210:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
211: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
212: - Could not put tone for other cases.

progress.message:
1) TL;DR

Your positive matrix is strong, but it does not yet prove the rule is correctly bounded. Keep the exhaustive “valid cases” sweep, but add a small set of negative and boundary tests, and tighten assertions so you verify untouched spans too. Also keep trigger `U`/`u`; that is a high-value case, not redundant.

2) Recommended approach (simple path)

1. Keep the core positive sweep, but define its scope precisely.
   - Your shape coverage is right: both incomplete `ươ` forms are required.
   - Your case coverage is right: preserving original case on each vowel is part of the spec.
   - Your trigger coverage is right: all 14 trigger forms matter because the implementation will likely land in separate switch arms in `src/liblex.zig` around lines 304, 382, 384, 385, 430, 433, and 434.
   - Your tone idea is mostly right, but be explicit that you are covering linguistically valid one-tone syllables, not every representable engine state.

2. Tighten the per-case assertions.
   - Assert post-length as “pre-length plus 1,” not a seed-derived shorthand that could hide an off-by-one.
   - Assert every pre-existing span in the tested window that should remain unchanged, including any prefix consonant seed.
   - In the prefixed case, assert the modification index is offset by the prefix length, not just “the receiving span.”
   - Conceptually, you want to prove exactly one existing span gains horn, the other vowel span is unchanged, the prefix is unchanged, and the new trigger span is appended literally.

3. Add a small negative suite.
   The main gap in your plan is not positive coverage; it is applicability boundaries. Add at least these:
   - valid incomplete pair exists, but is not immediately before the trigger
   - both vowels have no horn
   - both vowels already have horn
   - wrong diacritic shape involved, such as circumflex instead of horn
   - input character not in the trigger list
   - optional but useful: processing is bypassed when `literal_index` is already set or `mode == .literal`

4. Add one overflow boundary test.
   - A very valuable case is when the incomplete pair sits at effective-buffer indices 14–15 and the trigger append pushes total length to 17.
   - That proves the rule still modifies the existing vowel correctly at the buffer edge and handles the literal append / `literal_index` transition correctly.
   - Without this, a broken implementation can pass your main matrix and still mishandle the only branch that both mutates an old span and appends beyond `buffer_effective`.

5. Split tests by purpose.
   - One exhaustive positive matrix test for valid cases
   - One focused negative-applicability test
   - One focused overflow / state-gating test

3) Rationale and trade-offs

- Your current plan is stronger than necessary on the positive path, but still incomplete overall because it doesn’t prove “only these cases trigger.”
- The highest-leverage additions are negative guards, not more positive permutations.
- The existing file already leans this way:
  - matrix-style positive tests at lines 691–809
  - then a focused interaction test at lines 1000–1137
- So a split suite fits existing convention better than one giant everything-test.

A specific note on tones:
- Your “6 tones on each vowel position with the other at level” is a good practical interpretation of valid syllables.
- But it does duplicate the all-level case.
- It also excludes malformed double-toned states, which the engine data model can represent even if the spec wouldn’t call them valid.
- That is fine if you say so explicitly. If you claim “all possible valid cases,” you are okay. If you claim “all possible representable states,” you are not.

4) Risks and guardrails

- Biggest proof gap: over-eager auto-fill.
  - A buggy implementation that fills on non-triggers, non-immediate pairs, or wrong diacritics would still pass your current positive-only plan.
- Biggest assertion gap: untouched-prefix corruption.
  - In your seeded scenario, if the implementation rewrites an earlier span, your listed assertions may miss it unless you explicitly inspect that prefix span.
- Biggest boundary gap: buffer overflow interaction.
  - Auto-fill is unusual because it both mutates an existing span and appends a literal trigger. That makes the index-16 boundary worth testing directly.
- `literal_index == null` is correct for ordinary successful auto-fill cases, but not for the overflow boundary case. So don’t hard-code that expectation into every scenario.

5) When to consider the advanced path

Use a more orthogonal test design only if the big sweep becomes painful to compile or hard to debug:
- very high branch quota
- slow test compile times
- failure output too noisy to localize quickly

6) Optional advanced path

If compile-time cost gets annoying, reduce positive redundancy before dropping negative coverage:
- keep one exhaustive tone/case/shape sweep with a representative trigger
- add a smaller trigger-membership sweep with representative tone/case
- keep the negative and overflow tests intact

Direct answers to your questions:

- Does this prove all valid cases?
  - It proves the transformation well for valid inputs, but not the applicability boundaries. So: not yet.

- Boundary scenarios to add?
  - Yes: especially the index 14–15 / length 16 boundary.
  - `literal_index` already set and `mode == .literal` are secondary but worthwhile as one representative guard test.
  - Cover trigger `U` specifically: yes, absolutely keep it.

- Are your assertions sufficient?
  - Almost, but not quite. Add assertions for every untouched existing span in the setup window, especially the prefix seed. That closes the main hole.

- Negative cases the spec says should not auto-fill?
  - Yes: no horn anywhere, both horns already present, wrong diacritic shape, non-trigger input, and pair not immediately before the input.

- One big sweep or multiple tests?
  - Multiple tests. Keep one big positive sweep, but split negatives and boundary behavior into smaller focused tests. That is the better fit for this file’s existing style around lines 691–809 and 1000–1137.

content.text:
## Synthesis: revised plan

The Oracle confirms the positive sweep but flags three real gaps. Here is the consolidated plan I'd like you to confirm before I write any code.

### Test 1 — `expect State.add auto-fill missing horn in valid cases` (positive sweep)

Scope: prove the transformation is correct for every valid `ươ` completion.

Matrix:
- 2 pair shapes (`uơ`, `ưo`).
- 4 case combos per shape (`uo, uO, Uo, UO`) → 8 starting pairs.
- 6 tones swept on each vowel position (other at `.level`) → 12 tone scenarios per pair (Vietnamese single-tone). Acknowledge in a comment that this covers valid syllables, not arbitrary engine states.
- 14 trigger characters (`C/c, I/i, M/m, N/n, P/p, T/t, U/u`).
- 2 position seeds: pair at indices `[0,1]`, and pair at `[1,2]` after a single consonant span (e.g. seeded `'c'`). Assert the consonant prefix span is unchanged.

Per-case assertions:
- `buffer_length == pre_length + 1` (computed from the recorded pre-length, not a constant).
- `buffer_modification_index == prefix_len + (0 for uơ | 1 for ưo)`.
- `literal_index == null`.
- `mode == .telex`.
- The receiving span: original base preserved, `.horn`, original tone preserved.
- The other vowel span: original base preserved, `.horn` (unchanged), original tone preserved.
- The prefix consonant span (when present): fully unchanged.
- The new trailing span: trigger char preserved, `.empty`, `.level`.

### Test 2 — negative applicability guards

One focused test that cycles through cases the spec says must NOT trigger auto-fill. For each, assert the implementation falls back to literal append (or whatever the existing per-character switch arm dictates) and does NOT mutate either preceding vowel:
- Both vowels lack horn (`uo`).
- Both vowels already have horn (`ươ`).
- Wrong diacritic shape (e.g. `uô`, `ôo`, `âo`).
- Trigger character not in the list (e.g. `b`, `q`, `k`).
- Valid pair not immediately before the trigger (e.g. seed `uơb`, then trigger `n` — auto-fill must not retro-fix the now-non-adjacent `uơ`).
- One representative guard each for `literal_index` already set and `mode == .literal`.

### Test 3 — overflow boundary

Seed so the incomplete pair sits at indices 14–15, then send a trigger. Assert:
- The horn is filled on the correct existing span at index 14 or 15.
- A new span is appended (trailing length goes to 17).
- `literal_index` becomes 16 (per existing overflow convention in the `else => { ... }` arm at lines 518–531) — explicitly NOT `null` here.
- `buffer_modification_index` is the modified vowel index, not `null`, despite the overflow.

### Conventions
- One test per concern; matches the file's existing split between matrix tests (lines 691–809) and focused interaction tests (lines 1000–1137).
- `inline for` over comptime tuples; bump `@setEvalBranchQuota` as needed.
- Seed via `Span.init_diacritic_tone`; never mutate a `Span`.
- Fresh `var state: State = undefined; state.init(); state.mode = .telex;` per case.

Confirm or amend, and I will write the tests.

Good idea for Test 2, Test 3. Here is my comment for them:

- Test 1: I agree with you.
- Test 2: We should select the seed naturally: `uo`, `ươ`, `uô`, trigger character not in the list (select a few cases).
- Test 3: I agree with you, good point.

Continue the discussion.

content.thinking:
The user wants Test 2 to focus on cleaner negative cases — characters without horns, characters that already have horns, wrong diacritics, and a few non-trigger characters. They're also dropping the "valid pair not immediately before trigger" case and the guards around `literal_index` and `mode`. I'm reconsidering whether those guards are actually necessary.

The existing literal-switch test already proves that once `literal_index` is set, subsequent inputs append literally, so the gate should be covered there. But the new trigger handlers (C, I, M, N, P, T, U) aren't tested in that literal-switch scenario yet. For Test 2, I'll stick with the natural scope the user outlined and then figure out what happens when a non-trigger character like 'b' or 'q' follows a valid auto-fill seed like `uơ`. I need to be careful about which characters are truly safe for Test 2 — some like 'w' and 'o' would actually trigger mutations on the previous span through other Telex rules, so they're not valid non-triggers. I should stick with characters that have no special handling at all, like 'b', 'k', 'h', 'l', 'q', or 'g', which will reliably fall through to the literal append branch. Let me use 'b' and 'k' as my test cases since they're unambiguous, and then move on to thinking through the overflow boundary scenario for Test 3.

For the overflow test, I'm setting up a seed with 14 literal characters filling the buffer, then manually constructing the vowel pair at positions 14-15 using diacritics directly rather than trying to build them through state transitions. When I trigger with a character like 'n', it should push the buffer length to 17, which exceeds the effective buffer capacity, so the new span gets appended to the literal section instead. I need to verify that the buffer_modification_index points to the right span, literal_index gets set correctly to mark where the overflow happened, and the existing diacritics remain untouched while the new character lands in the overflow area.

For the prefix-consonant variant of Test 1, I'll seed with 'b' characters instead of 'c' to avoid any ambiguity about whether the seed character itself triggers special logic — this keeps the test focused purely on the auto-fill behavior when the pair is already in place.

content.text:
## Refined plan based on your guidance

### Test 2 — refined to natural seeds

Single test "expect State.add does not auto-fill horn for invalid cases".

Per-case state setup is the same shape: seed two spans, trigger, then assert literal append with both seed spans untouched.

Cases:
1. **`uo`** — both vowels empty (no horn anywhere). Trigger: a representative auto-fill character like `n`. Expect literal append, no mutation.
2. **`ươ`** — both already have horn (nothing to fill). Trigger: `n`. Expect literal append, no mutation.
3. **`uô`** — wrong diacritic on the second vowel (circumflex, not horn). Trigger: `n`. Expect literal append, no mutation.
4. **`uơ` + non-trigger character** — pick characters that are non-triggers AND don't fire any other Telex rule against `ơ`. Safe picks: `b` and `k`. (Avoid `w`, `o`, `s/f/r/x/j/z` because they would interact with `ơ` via horn-cancel, circumflex-override, or tone arms and would muddy the assertion.)

Per-case assertions:
- `buffer_length == 3`.
- `buffer_modification_index == null`.
- `literal_index == null`.
- `mode == .telex`.
- Both seed spans verified unchanged (base, diacritic, tone).
- Trailing span: trigger preserved, `.empty`, `.level`.

I'm dropping the "valid pair not immediately before trigger" and the "`literal_index` already set / `mode == .literal`" guards as you requested. The literal-mode/literal_index gating is already proved generally by the existing literal-switch test (lines 1000–1137); the new switch arms will inherit the same gate at the top of each branch.

### Test 1 — minor tweak to the prefix variant

For the "pair preceded by a consonant" position seed, I'll use `b` (a clean non-trigger, non-Telex consonant) rather than `c` so the prefix span carries no special meaning and any mutation of it would be unambiguous.

### Test 3 — overflow boundary, finalized

Single test "expect State.add auto-fills horn at buffer_effective boundary".

Setup:
- Seed `buffer_effective[0..14]` with literal `b` spans (via `Span.init_diacritic_tone(b, .empty, .level)`).
- Seed indices 14 and 15 with the incomplete pair (one variant per shape: `uơ` and `ưo`, two sub-cases).
- `buffer_length = 16`, `mode = .telex`, `literal_index = null`.
- Trigger: `n`.

Assertions:
- `buffer_length == 17`.
- `buffer_modification_index == 14` (for `uơ`) or `15` (for `ưo`).
- `literal_index == 16` — the trailing append exceeds the effective buffer, so the existing convention from the `else` arm (lines 518–531) must apply.
- `mode == .telex`.
- The receiving span at index 14 or 15 is mutated to `.horn`, base and tone preserved.
- The other vowel span unchanged.
- Spans 0..14 unchanged (verify all 14).
- No assertion on the trailing span contents — `append_literal` (lines 541–552) only writes when `buffer_length < buffer_effective.len`, so index 16 is intentionally not stored.

### Implementation order

I'll write all three tests directly after the existing literal-switch test (replacing the current empty stub at line 1139). Test 1 first (matrix), then Test 2 (negatives), then Test 3 (boundary).

Confirm to proceed and I will write the code.
I agree with your plan. Write the tests.

Looking at how existing tests handle similar complexity, I see they use `@setEvalBranchQuota` to bump the limit up to around 2,000 or higher depending on the iteration count. For my case, I'm estimating I'll need somewhere in the 10,000–20,000 range given the nested loops and assertion overhead.

I also need to handle prefix variations (with or without 'b'), which multiplies the test count to around 2688 iterations. I'll bump up the compile-time evaluation quota to handle this volume.

Now I'm defining the pair cases as a tuple of structs, each specifying the base characters, diacritics, and which vowel receives the horn during auto-fill.

Setting up test parameters... Continuing with the test assertions...

I'm verifying that the prefix 'b' remains unchanged when present, then checking that both vowel spans now have the horn diacritic applied while preserving their original case and tone values. The new trigger character should be appended literally to the buffer with no diacritic or tone modification. I'm also thinking through the type considerations — `tpos` as a comptime integer should work fine with the inline for loop, and since `plen` and `pc.receiving_offset` are both comptime values, the `receiving_index` calculation should resolve correctly at compile time.

For the equality checks, I need to handle the optional type `?u8` for `state.buffer_modification_index`. The testing framework should coerce the comptime integer values appropriately, similar to how existing tests pass literal 0 values. Same approach applies for `buffer_length` — the comptime sum `plen + 3` should coerce to `u8` without issue. Let me check the Zig version being used to confirm the type coercion behavior.

I'm writing the three test cases now, replacing the stub at line 1139. I need to verify that the type coercion works correctly—a comptime int should coerce to `?u8` in the expectEqual call—and then check that the `else` branch for literals properly increments the literal_index counter.

Looking at the overflow behavior: when buffer_length reaches 16 and we append another character, it becomes 17, which exceeds the buffer_effective array bounds (indices 0-15). content.text:
All three tests compile and run as expected. They fail with the precise messages you'd expect for an unimplemented feature:

- **Test 1** (valid cases): trigger `'C'` falls into the empty `'C', 'c' => {}` arm at [src/liblex.zig:304](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L304), so `buffer_length` stays at 2 instead of 3.
- **Test 2** (invalid cases): same root cause — trigger `'n'` hits the empty `'N', 'n' => {}` arm at [src/liblex.zig:385](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L385).
- **Test 3** (boundary): same — `'n'` arm is empty.

The other 19 tests still pass, confirming no regression.

Tests written and failing for the right reason. Ready for the implementation phase.
